data.title: How chaotic do your fellow passengers need to be for an airline to give you compensation?
data.description:
By Aditi Shrikant

One flier told MarketWatch that a fellow passenger sprayed them with perfume. She contacted the airline's CEO.

"Whether you're on Delta or Spirit, I don't think there is that much of a difference anymore," travel blogger Jessica Nabongo says. (Photo subjects are models.)

Kiki Monique considers herself a Delta Air Lines loyalist after flying with the carrier for more than a decade. Sitting in first class on a flight in 2023, however, Monique, who hosts a podcast about reality-TV news, ended up the victim of some Bravo-like drama herself.

"This passenger sitting behind me kept accusing me of passing gas - and it was not the case," she said. "She was being very loud about it. It was so embarrassing and disruptive, and she kept calling the flight attendants over."

Monique felt a mist fall over her and realized the passenger was spraying her down with perfume. She could smell the scent in her clothes and hair as she got off the plane.

"It was mortifying," she said. "Imagine being accused of something you're not doing, and something as disgusting as that. As if I'm some animal that can't go to the bathroom."

'It was mortifying.'Kiki Monique, who says another airline passenger harassed her and sprayed her with perfume

The flight attendants tried to appease and quiet the complaining passenger. And while Monique didn't want to draw any more attention to herself, she did want Delta (DAL) to know how uncomfortable she felt.

She called the customer-support line, but they weren't helpful. She then posted on social media about her experience, and someone commented that Delta CEO Ed Bastian sometimes answers emails directly. She sent him a message and, to her surprise, he responded. "He spoke to me for about 20 minutes and was very apologetic," she said.

Delta refunded her the cost of her flight and gave her 100,000 miles.

Cases like Monique's highlight that gray area between passengers behaving badly, your own feelings of safety as a passenger, and official U.S. Transportation Department guidelines for claiming compensation.

"The top priority for every Delta flight attendant is to ensure the safety and comfort of all customers while providing exceptional service," a Delta spokesperson told MarketWatch in an email. "Flight attendants undergo extensive, ongoing training to manage a wide range of onboard scenarios."

Increased anxiety around flying

While the Transportation Department doesn't explicitly state that passengers should be compensated for subpar flight experiences, travelers like Monique believe that requests for such compensation are warranted.

"When there is a lot of anxiety in the world, there tends to be more anxiety on the aircraft," said Josh Rosenberg, a flight attendant and the second vice president of TWU Local 556, the labor union for Southwest Airlines (LUV) flight attendants.

And the world right now can feel a bit chaotic as a result of the angst created by the war against Iran, coupled with economic uncertainty at home and a surge in oil prices.

Flight attendants are there primarily to ensure safety, Rosenberg said, not to referee social dynamics: "There should not be an expectation that flight attendants will settle armrest disputes."

But neither should passengers hesitate to approach a flight attendant about their comfort. "It's always OK to go to a flight attendant. Sometimes they can help," he said. "They might let you stand in the back with them. We can't change your neighbor's personality, but we can try to make you more comfortable."

There are Transportation Department rules rules about flight refunds and other forms of compensation. For instance, if bags are lost or "significantly delayed" - by at least 12 hours for a domestic flight or by 15 to 30 hours for an international trip, depending on its duration - customers are entitled to a refund of their baggage fee.

In many situations, however, the rules vary by airline. The government agency has a chart outlining what kind of compensation each major airline offers customers.

Last March, an American Airlines flight made an emergency landing in Detroit due to an unruly passenger.

Many disruptive incidents don't neatly fall into any specific scenario. In November 2025, an American Airlines (AAL) flight en route from Phoenix to Washington, D.C., was forced to make an emergency landing because of a drunk, disruptive passenger. The airline released a statement at the time, saying "law enforcement met the flight and removed the customer, and the flight later redeparted."

Last month, another American Airlines flight, from New York to Chicago, made an emergency landing in Detroit because an unruly passenger was demanding that the plane land. At the time, American Airlines issued a statement about a "disruptive customer," saying that "law enforcement and medical personnel met the flight and the customer deplaned. We appreciate our customers for their patience and thank our crew for their professionalism."

American Airlines did not respond to MarketWatch for a request for comment on these incidents, and whether passengers were offered any compensation.

There is, of course, no accounting for other passengers. In 2019, Jessica Nabongo - a travel blogger and public speaker who says she has visited every country in the world - was on a Alitalia (now known as ITA Airways) flight from Rome to Tel Aviv, when a passenger refused to sit next to her.

"He cited religious reasons," she recalled. "The flight attendant asked me if I would move, and I said, 'No. This is a commercial plane. I'm not moving to accommodate someone who is claiming they won't sit next to me because of their religion.'"

Ultimately, the crew resat him elsewhere, but the ordeal took some time. "It held up our flight since I refused to move from my assigned seat," Nabongo said. She thought about asking for a refund, but, in this instance, decided against it.

Time to shop around?

The Federal Aviation Administration has strict rules and a "zero-tolerance" policy regarding unruly passengers. Interfering with crew and making threats of violence can result in severe civil penalties and criminal prosecution.

"The rate of unruly-passenger incidents steadily dropped by over 80% since record highs in early 2021, but recent increases show there remains more work to do," the FAA says on its website.

Yet the number of incidents involving unruly passengers has not gone unnoticed. The FAA says "incidents where airline passengers have disrupted flights with threatening or violent behavior are an ongoing problem."

Nabongo said she hasn't always had positive experiences with flight crews or passengers, in part due to the fact that she travels so frequently. Nabongo is a Delta Diamond Medallion member - the top tier of Delta fliers - but she doesn't always believe she is treated like one.

She once received a refund after a flight attendant made a disparaging remark about how she was dressed. "I was wearing this matching set and a bralette you could partially see, and the flight attendant said to me, 'They let you board like that? They let you get on the plane like that?'" she recalled.

When the flight attendant saw where she was sitting, Nabongo said, she took a friendlier tone. "She kept coming back and trying to compliment me, like, 'Oh my god, I love your nails,'" she said. "I asked, 'What's your name?' and I absolutely filed a complaint."

'Airlines don't want to get bad PR.'Meghna Maharishi, an airline expert at Skift

In another instance, Nabongo had a first-class ticket but didn't board with that group. She tried boarding using the Sky Priority line - but upon showing her ticket, she was told she had to go to the back of the line, even though Delta policy states that passengers sitting in first class can board ahead of economy passengers using the faster queue. Nabongo complained, and got a partial refund.

Experiences like this have encouraged Nabongo to shop around more, she says, despite her elite status with Delta. "Loyalty to an airline isn't as important as it used to be," she said. "Whether you're on Delta or Spirit, I don't think there is that much of a difference anymore. It's more important to do what makes sense and is affordable, and focus on getting points and miles via your credit card."

If you do want to file a complaint, first look at the airline's contract of carriage. That's the often-overlooked written agreement with the airline that a passenger signs off on when they purchase a ticket. It spells out what behavior the company deems acceptable, and what it doesn't.

Delta's contract of carriage states that it may remove a passenger if their "conduct is disorderly, abusive or violent, or the passenger appears to be intoxicated or under the influence of drugs." United Airlines' (UAL) contract also says it can remove passengers "who are barefoot, not properly clothed, or whose clothing is lewd, obscene or offensive." Similarly, American Airlines says it will remove passengers who are "uncooperative, abusive, harassing, or show the potential to be while onboard."

However, loyalty does make airlines more likely to offer a refund, said Meghna Maharishi, an airline expert at Skift, a platform focused on travel-industry intelligence. "If you have status on an airline, they are more obligated to give you something if you have a bad experience, because they do care about that," she said.

Even if you don't have frequent-flier status, negotiating some sort of reimbursement is still worth it, Maharishi said, as it's in an airline's best interest to appease disgruntled customers.

"Airlines don't want to get bad PR - and they tend to get a lot of bad PR already on a daily basis with crazy flight experiences," she said. "There is some minor pressure to avoid that."

-Aditi Shrikant

(MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires

04-10-26 1801ET

Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Bovespa Index Ends the Week 4.93% Higher at 197323.87 — Data Talk
data.description:

     --Largest one-week point and percentage gain since the week ending Jan 23, 2026 



     --Up for three consecutive weeks

data.title: Financials Down as Private-Credit Worries Simmer — Financials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of banks and other financial institutions fell as private-credit worries simmered ahead of earnings in the sector.

Goldman Sachs is set to report earnings on Monday. JPMorgan Chase, Citigroup and Wells Fargo will follow Tuesday. Investors are awaiting the banks' views of how Iran war and private-credit scares will affect broader financial and economic activity.

Shares of private-credit firm Blue Owl hovered around recent lows. Large banks including JPMorgan Chase are preparing to offer a new way for investors to bet against managers of private-credit funds.

The CDX Financials credit-default swap index will include private-credit funds managed by Apollo Global Management, Ares Management and Blackstone, comprising 12% of the index, which also includes insurers, regional banks and credit-card companies.

It's another echo of the mortgage meltdown in 2008, when credit-default swap indices were closely watched.

Research firm A.M. Best warned the investment portfolios of insurers that sell annuities hold more risky debt than they did in 2007, the year before the worst downturn since the Great Depression.

Life insurers' risk of failure to pay claims is rising, the firm warned.

The day after President Trump announced a sudden pause of strikes against Iran last month, the White House warned staff against improperly leveraging their positions to place well-timed bets in futures markets. 




  Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com 



  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 10, 2026 17:47 ET (21:47 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Consumer Cos Flat on Hopes for Strait Reopening — Consumer Roundup
data.description:
Consumer companies were more or less flat amid fears that the energy shock caused by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz would continue.

Consumer sentiment fell in April to the lowest level recorded in the 70-plus-year history of the University of Michigan's survey, evidence of Americans' concerns that the Iran war will hit the domestic economy.

"We still expect cuts this year given the Fed's bias to look through supply-driven inflation, little signs of wage pressures, and political pressure," said economists at brokerage Bank of America Global Research, in a note to clients. The energy shock could prove to have a greater influence on growth outlook than on the inflation outlook, the BofA economists said. "By September, Warsh should be in and have enough evidence of inflation cooling to rally support for a couple of cuts."

Nike's chief innovation officer, Tony Bignell, is leaving after less than a year in the job, the company said, amid signs that its turnaround efforts are sputtering. 




  Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com 



  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 10, 2026 17:40 ET (21:40 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: AI spending will boost productivity - but first it'll make even the most efficient firms look like slackers
data.description:
By Laura McGee and Andrea Bonime-Blanc

Companies and investors can expect a painful transition - and the economy will be impacted as well

Companies aren't getting much of a boost from AI. If anything, the opposite is true.

Most firms use AI along with existing processes rather than replace them. This creates short term duplication and confusion at best - and mid- to long-term losses, at worst.

There's a broad consensus right now that artificial intelligence will make investment firms - and the companies they back - more efficient. The expectation is that AI agents will handle the time-consuming work: synthesizing information, drafting materials and identifying patterns. Humans move up the stack - focusing on judgment, strategy and relationships.

The economics are compelling. Estimates suggest AI could drive meaningful productivity gains across asset management, with McKinsey projecting double-digit improvements in margins as workflows become more automated.

But there's a disconnect between that narrative and what's actually happening.

Most firms aren't seeing a productivity lift yet. If anything, they're seeing the opposite. Some because they're in the process of building their AI carefully and meticulously, but others because they don't know what they're doing with AI.

The dip no one is pricing in

There's a well-documented pattern with new technologies. Productivity doesn't rise immediately. First it dips.

There's a well-documented pattern with new technologies. Productivity doesn't rise immediately. First, it dips.

Investors experienced this with electricity. Again with computers. Again with the internet.

AI is following the same path.

Right now, most firms are operating in a hybrid state - using AI alongside existing processes rather than replacing them. This creates short-term duplication and confusion at best - and mid- to long-term losses, at worst.

In practice, many firms will go through a period of six to 18 months where productivity stalls or declines as they experiment, rebuild workflows and figure out what actually works. And that's in the better-case scenario where a firm knows (or kind of knows) what it is doing.

In our work, we see these two scenarios unfolding firsthand: One of us advises corporate boards on what to watch for in AI; the other embeds AI into investor workflows.

At one $20 billion investment fund we worked with, introducing AI into the investment memo process led to a fivefold increase in time spent - at least initially. A memo that used to take around 10 hours ended up taking more than 50.

The team had to map the full workflow, document domain expertise, structure internal data, build agents and then verify everything. Outputs couldn't be trusted blindly. Every assumption had to be tested.

And at the partner level, there were ongoing debates about whether any of it was credible enough to rely on. This is what real adoption looks like early on.

The other scenario - fake or no adoption - occurs when the board and the CEO don't really have an AI-adoption plan, don't have the right people internally and/or externally to guide them, don't understand or address their own data and tech architecture - and in some extreme cases, think AI is just a bubble and will go away. That's what non-adoption or "head in the sand" adoption looks like.

Why productivity drops before it rises

In the better-case scenario, productivity drops before it rises for several reasons - none having to do with the technology itself.

1. Data isn't ready: Most firms are sitting on years of unstructured information - documents, models, notes - that weren't designed for machines. Before AI can be useful, that data has to be cleaned, tagged and organized. That's manual, time-consuming work.

2. Workflows were never explicit: A lot of high-value work depends on judgment that's never been formally defined. AI forces teams to spell it out. What are the steps? What matters most? Where are the decision points? Such clarity takes time.

3. Validation slows everything down: AI outputs look convincing, even when they're wrong. Early on, teams have to check everything. That adds friction, but it's necessary to understand where the system can be trusted.

4. Old systems don't disappear: Few firms switch overnight. They run AI next to legacy processes, which means duplication. Two systems, one outcome.

5. People don't move at the same speed: Some adopt these processes quickly. Others are skeptical. Most sit somewhere in the middle. That inconsistency creates bottlenecks and inconsistency across teams.

Individually, these are manageable. Together, they create a drag on productivity.

The investor problem

From the outside, the adoption (or "fake" adoption) phase is hard to read.

A firm going through a real transformation may show higher costs, slower output and more internal friction. That can look like underperformance.

At the same time, a firm doing very little can maintain steady metrics in the short term.

So the question becomes: Is this company investing through the transition, or avoiding it?

What to watch for

Serious teams don't assume AI is reliable. They build systems to test it, challenge it, and improve it over time.

Start with specificity. The signals aren't in the headlines or even the productivity metrics. They're in the details. Firms doing real work can point to the workflows they're changing and why. Vague references to "AI integration" don't tell you much.

Look at where time and money are going. The heavy lift is usually in data and process, not just tools.

Pay attention to how management talks about productivity. If the metrics haven't changed, the underlying system probably hasn't either.

And listen to how they handle trust. Serious teams don't assume AI is reliable. They build systems to test, challenge and improve it over time.

Finally, find out what the firm has presented to its C-suite and board on AI adoption. Much can be gleaned from such presentations. Apply the reasons why productivity declines before it rises - and you'll understand the truth about the AI adoption (or not) taking place at the firm.

How firms get through the dip faster

Some firms will move through this phase more quickly than others.

The ones that do tend to focus on a few things early. They invest in data before they invest in tools. Without structured information, AI doesn't have much to work with. They map workflows clearly, instead of dropping AI into existing processes and hoping for improvement.

They test and retest the data for quality and build quality and risk guardrails into their mapped workflows, instead of hoping for the best. They spend time on adoption. Not just training people on how to use the tools, but building a shared understanding of what AI can and can't do today. That reduces both overconfidence and skepticism.

And they report this information to executive management and the board in quantitative and qualitative ways, so that leadership can oversee the details and the firm's overall direction for AI.

None of this is particularly flashy. It's operational work.

What happens next

The benefits from AI will come through, but not right away. In the near term, firms doing the real work on AI may actually look less efficient than their peers. That reverses over time. The ones that have rebuilt how they operate will start to pull ahead. This advantage won't be hard to see.

The challenge for management, directors and investors is to have patience through this painstaking process while simultaneously kicking the tires and asking all the right questions during the building process. Only those who do this will end up long-term AI winners.

Laura McGee is co-founder of Arterra AI, a decision intelligence platform that helps investors gain alpha through AI. Andrea Bonime-Blanc is founder of GEC Risk Advisory and author of "Governing Pandora: Leading in the Age of Generative AI and Exponential Technology" (Georgetown University Press, 2026).

Plus: The AI trade is reawakening. The timing couldn't be better.

More: The smart money is plugging into these power-sector stocks - and opening new frontiers for AI-driven growth

-Laura McGee -Andrea Bonime-Blanc

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  04-10-26 1741ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Company Headlines at 5 PM ET: Nike Switches Up Its Innovation Leadership | CoreWeave, ...
data.description:
Nike Switches Up Its Innovation Leadership

Company veteran Tony Bignell is leaving the sneaker giant after less than a year as chief innovation officer as its turnaround runs into snags.

----

CoreWeave, Anthropic Form AI Cloud Agreement

CoreWeave will use its cloud infrastructure to help run Anthropic's Claude artificial-intelligence models as part of a new partnership between the two companies.

----

TotalEnergies, Aramco Refinery Shut After Being Damaged

The Saudi Aramco Total Refining and Petrochemical Co., or Satorp, is a joint venture with TotalEnergies, which holds a 37.5% interest.

----

Alibaba's New AI Video-Generation Model Tops Global Ranking

The model, called HappyHorse 1.0, is leading a global ranking that tracks AI models' abilities.

----

Meta Banks on AI to Clear the Smoke of Social-Media Lawsuits

While the tech giant has the means to fight in court, ongoing legal battles could temper a long-term recovery in its shares.

----

Insurers Take Bigger Risks Than Before 2008-09 Crisis, Report Warns

An industry praised for its resilience is now "significantly worse off" as it invests huge sums in private credit, A.M. Best said.

----

Replimune Stock Tumbles After FDA Rejects Skin Cancer Therapy for Second Time

Replimune Group submitted its first Biologics License Application for RP1 last year.

----

Florida AG Investigates OpenAI, ChatGPT, Citing National Security Risks, FSU Shooting

Florida Attorney General James Uthmeier raised concerns that OpenAI's models or data could be used by adversaries of America, namely China.

----

How Apple Can Win Back Gen-Z With Neo

Apple has been posting a variety of new TikTok videos. They could be just what's needed to lift the Neo.

----

SpaceX Is Going Public. Why a Tesla Merger Could Be Musk's Real Endgame.

From the biggest IPO on record to the largest M&A deal in history?

----

Is MSG Maker Ajinomoto Sitting on an AI Goldmine? This Investor Thinks So

An activist investor has built a stake in a Japanese company it says has a lucrative monopoly on a material vital to artificial-intelligence infrastructure.

----

Sazerac Eyes Deal With Jack Daniel's Maker Brown-Forman

Interest comes as Pernod Ricard has been discussing a mostly stock deal with Brown-Forman.

----

CarMax Will Add Two Board Members After Talks With Activist Investor Starboard

The move comes after Starboard urged the used-car retailer's new chief executive officer, Keith Barr, to revamp its pricing framework, streamline its digital processes, and cut costs. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 10, 2026 17:15 ET (21:15 GMT)

data.title: Trump Administration Announces Companies to Receive Oil in Latest SPR Release — OPIS
data.description:
Four companies have been selected as winning bidders for 8.5 million bbl of crude from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, the Energy Department said Friday.

The awards are from the second round of bidding for what is expected to be the release of a total of 172 million bbl from the stockpile in coming weeks.

The latest round saw four companies awarded contracts. Phillips 66 will receive 2.9 million bbl, Trafigura Trading 2.48 million bbl, Macquarie Commodities Trading US 2 million bbl and Gunvor USA LLC 1.1 million bbl.

The oil is being released through a return-exchange program, under which oil is loaned to companies that will return it by next year, along with additional barrels as a premium for the exchange. The DOE award announcement didn't include information about the premium called for in the contracts and DOE didn't respond to an inquiry Friday.

The oil in the latest release will come from the reserve's Bryan Mound, Texas, site.

The previous round of the return exchange involved 45.22 million bbl.

On Thursday, DOE solicited bids for a third exchange return of 30 million bbl from the reserve's site in West Hackberry, La. Bids for that release are due by 11 a.m. CDT Monday.

The SPR release is part of a coordinated effort by member nations of the International Energy Agency. The releases are to alleviate supply disruptions caused by Iran's effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz following attacks by the U.S. and Israel that began Feb. 28. The U.S. release is less than half the 400 million bbl planned to be removed from global strategic stockpiles during the program. 




  This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 





  -- Reporting by Steve Cronin, scronin@opisnet.com; Editing by Michael Kelly,  mkelly@opisnet.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 10, 2026 17:21 ET (21:21 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Investors are gloomy about inflation and risk-taking ahead of Saturday's Iran talks
data.description:
By Joy Wiltermuth

The economic toll of the Iran conflict is starting to show up at home

The Dow and S&P 500 pulled back Friday following a rally earlier in the week, as the U.S. and Iran prepare to meet Saturday to discuss their fragile truce.

This week's big rally in stocks lost some steam Friday amid rising inflation at home and as Saturday's planned U.S.-Iran meeting in Islamabad put the countries' fragile cease-fire in focus.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA shed 0.6%, the S&P 500 SPX edged 0.1% lower and Nasdaq Composite COMP gained 0.4% on the day.

"Each day, the news is changing," said Max Wasserman, founder and senior portfolio manager at Miramar Capital. "I'm not convinced that Iran and the Middle East is going to turn out rosy in a short period of time."

Instead, he expects hiccups along the way, elevated oil prices and U.S. inflation to stay higher for longer. "You are going to find that interest rates and inflation are the new issue after this calms down with the Middle East," Wasserman said.

President Trump helped darken the mood Friday, saying in a social-media post that "Iranians don't seem to realize they have no cards, other than a short-term extortion of the world by using international waterways."

Trump was referring to Tehran's plans to charge tolls on vessels passing through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint for the flow of Middle East oil, fertilizer and much more.

See: Here's how much money Iran can make charging tolls on one-fifth of the world's seaborne oil

Hormuz tolls could be a valuable source of new income for Iran but they also would increase the oil risk premium, or the extra cost embedded in a barrel of oil to offset things like supply shocks.

U.S. (CL00) and global (BRN00) crude-oil prices crashed lower for the week after a temporary cease-fire deal was announced, but were still around $95 a barrel on Friday.

Elevated energy costs could make the U.S. inflation problem worse - potentially through the summer - while Americans already have been facing $4-a-gallon gas prices at the pump.

That's the backdrop against which Vice President J.D. Vance and a U.S. delegation are set to meet with Iranian officials in the Pakistani capital of Islamabad starting on Saturday. A good outcome might simply be a continuation of talks.

Read: What's at stake for markets as the U.S. and Iran talk this weekend

Bond yields rose Friday ahead of the talks, but also after the consumer-price index for March showed inflation touching a two-year high of 3.3% annually - a level that reflected the spike in oil prices during the Iran war.

The increase in yields pushed the all-important 10-year Treasury rate BX:TMUBMUSD10Y back up to 4.316%. That's off its recent peak, but still well above its dip below 4% before the Iran conflict was started by the U.S. and Israel on Feb. 28. The 2-year Treasury yield BX:TMUBMUSD02Y briefly traded above 4% in late March, but has since eased back to 3.8%.

Tony Rodriguez, head of fixed-income strategy at Nuveen, called this week's action a "relief rally and a positioning rally," with positioning around "some dire scenarios" around the Iran war and the oil shock being unwound.

Yet today's higher yields put upward pressure on borrowing costs, including 30-year mortgage rates. This comes as rising inflation increases prices, but also creates a difficult backdrop for the Federal Reserve to justify additional interest-rate cuts.

Rodriguez called it an "OK environment" - one where increased energy prices aren't expected to lead to a U.S. recession, but will likely mean weaker economic growth of about 1.8%.

Still, the broader inflation picture - and what gets passed on to consumers in terms of prices - will depend on how long oil stays around $100 a barrel and whether the truce in the Middle East holds, Rodriguez said. Those forces make it hard to take aggressively bullish or bearish positioning ahead of Saturday's planned talks, he added.

Despite the Dow's pullback Friday, it still gained 3% for the week, while the S&P 500 jumped 3.6% on the week and the Nasdaq gained 4.7%, according to FactSet data.

"I think the market is a little too optimistic," Miramar's Wasserman said.

-Joy Wiltermuth

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  04-10-26 1707ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Industrials Down on Anxiety Ahead of Iran Talks — Industrials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of industrial and transportation companies fell amid anxiety ahead of critical talks between the U.S. and Iranian delegations in Islamabad.

Airlines, airports and fuel suppliers are warning that Tuesday's cease-fire deal, even if it holds, won't solve an increasingly acute jet-fuel shortage any time soon.

China has stopped exporting jet fuel so it can meet its own needs, leading to shortages in airports across Asia, including those in Vietnam and Myanmar.

Tesla is on pace to surpass 10 billion cumulative full-self driving miles by late May, said analysts at brokerage Morgan Stanley, the latest sign that the electric-vehicle maker's goal of fully automated robo-taxis is within reach. 




  Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com 



  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 10, 2026 17:08 ET (21:08 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.
